Backtesting using vectorbt — cookbook (Part 2)

Tobi Lux
19 min read
·
Feb 4, 2026

This is part-2 of a cookbook for using essential elements of vectorbt (vbt) for backtesting. Note: my statements are valid for the free version of vbt only (version 0.28.1).

As always, the full code is available on my GitHub — see also the disclaimer at the end.

Content of the blog

This series of blogs has the following content:

  • Part 1: Setting the scene, getting started with vbt, Overview on methods and parameters (for calculation of metrics, plotting, logging etc.)
  • Part 2: (this part) trade-prices (default/modified), impact of parameters as size_type, size, min_size, max_size, fees, slippage, standard StopLoss and TakeProfit-behaviour
  • Part 3: callback-functions in vbt, using callbacks for customizing Stops
  • Part 4 : multiple orders per position, impact of accumulation, accept_partial, StopLoss and TakeProfit for multiple orders, ShortOnly trades, LongShort trades
  • Part 5 : multi-ticker portfolio and standard outputs, cash distributions, cash-pooling (with and without order size limitations), groups of tickers, group-cash-pools (with and without order size-limitations), LongShortStrategies in multiticker-portfolios
  • Part 6: some use-cases as batch-testing of different strategies, optimisation of parameters of trading strategies, sector rotation, etc…

1. Data Download and Signal Generation

We apply the same methodology used in Part 1:

  • Download price data from yfinance
  • Use an SMA-crossing, long-only strategy
  • Build a single-ticker portfolio

Additionally, we extract all OHLC components into separate PandasSeries for later use, rather than relying only on close prices only, as in Part 1.

# download data
ticker = 'AAPL'
start_date = '2022-01-01'
end_date = '2025-10-11'
prices = yf.download(ticker, start=start_date, end=end_date, auto_adjust=True)

# preparing for ta & vbt-use (remove MultiLevel-column-index, transform to several pd.Series)
close = prices.Close[ticker]
open = prices.Open[ticker]
high = prices.High[ticker]
low = prices.Low[ticker]

# entry and exit signals via ta,
fast_window = 10
slow_window = 60

fast_sma = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close, window=fast_window)
slow_sma = ta.trend.sma_indicator(close, window=slow_window)

# Entry and Exit Signals by simple SMA-crossing
entries = ((fast_sma > slow_sma) & (fast_sma.shift(1) < slow_sma.shift(1))).rename(ticker)
exits = ((fast_sma < slow_sma) & (fast_sma.shift(1) > slow_sma.shift(1))).rename(ticker)

2. Default Trade-Prices for Entries and Exits

We start with the baseline-portfolio, already introduced in part 1.

# baseline portfolio
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

To evaluate the behaviour, we will have to execute three key tasks:

  • Tabulating executed trades and their key parameters
  • Creating a timeline (a DataFrame containing all relevant portfolio data)
  • Inspecting the timeline around entry and exit points

The first topic can be addressed using the built-in pf.trades.records_readable method.

For the second topic, we use the helper function get_timeline_basic(...) from our own helper module.

The resulting timeline DataFrame will include:

  • Dates (as the DataFrame index)
  • OHLC price data
  • Entry and exit signals (marked True when triggered)
  • Effective entries and exits (derived from the trade records, marked True only when an order is actually executed)

Why do we need effective entries and exits?
Because not all entry or exit signals result in executed trades. For example:

  • If we are already long and lack sufficient cash, an entry signal is ignored.
  • If we are already flat, an exit signal is ignored.

The timeline function is as follows:

def get_timeline_basic(prices: pd.DataFrame, entries: pd.Series, exits: pd.Series, trades: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    ''' Generates a timeline from OHLC-data, entries, exits from strategy and true trade data.
    Args:
        prices: OHLC prices.
        entires: signals from strategy, indicating entry points.
        exits: signals from strategy, indicating exit points.
        trades: trade records.
    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: DataFrame with plot parameters.
    '''
    # Flatten the column MultiIndex by dropping the 'Ticker' level (level 1)
    price_flat = prices.droplevel(1, axis=1)

    # Create the timeline DataFrame with the desired price columns
    timeline = pd.DataFrame({
        'open': price_flat['Open'],
        'high': price_flat['High'],
        'low': price_flat['Low'],
        'close': price_flat['Close'],
    }, index=prices.index)

    # Add system generated entries and exits signals
    timeline['sys_entries'] = entries
    timeline['sys_exits'] = exits

    # Generate eff_entries from Entry Timestamp
    eff_entries = pd.Series(False, index=prices.index)
    eff_entries.loc[trades['Entry Timestamp'].dropna()] = True

    # generates eff_exits from Exit Timestamp
    eff_exits = pd.Series(False, index=prices.index)
    eff_exits.loc[trades['Exit Timestamp'].dropna()] = True

    # Add the boolean series as columns to the plot_parameters DataFrame
    timeline['eff_entries'] = eff_entries
    timeline['eff_exits'] = eff_exits

    return timeline

Case by case, we can extend the timeline DataFrame with additional parameters.

In our example, we add:

  • The values of the fast SMA and slow SMA
  • The cash balance at each time step

With the code block below , we now generate:

  1. A DataFrame containing all trades with their key metrics
  2. The enriched timeline DataFrame
# key trade data
trades = pf.trades.records_readable

# timeline for standard + specific trading parameter
timeline=get_timeline_basic(prices, entries, exits, trades)     # standards (price, entry, exits)
timeline['sma_fast'] = fast_sma                                 # Adding fast-SMA & slow-SMA
timeline['sma_slow'] = slow_sma
timeline['cash'] = pf.cash()                                    # adding cash

The timeline can now be inspected by slicing the DataFrame for relevant periods using .loc or .iloc.

As an alternative, I created a helper function lense_at_entry_exit(...) that displays a “lens” view of the timeline around a selected entry and exit date—showing only the window from n bars before to n bars after each event.

def lense_at_entry_exit(timeline: pd.DataFrame, entry_date: str, exit_date: str, n_rows: int):
    ''' Slices and displays the timeline around a given entry_date and exit_date (+-n_rows)
    Args:
        timeline: The DataFrame containing the timeline data.
        entry_date: The entry date in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format.
        exit_date: The exit date in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format.
        n_rows: The number of rows to display before and after the entry/exit dates.
    '''
    # Convert date strings to datetime objects for accurate index lookup
    entry_ts = pd.Timestamp(entry_date)
    exit_ts = pd.Timestamp(exit_date)

    # --- Entry Date Analysis ---
    print(f"### Timeline around Entry Date: {entry_date} ({n_rows} rows before and after)\n")
    entry_loc = timeline.index.get_loc(entry_ts)
    start_entry_idx = max(0, entry_loc - n_rows)
    end_entry_idx = min(len(timeline) - 1, entry_loc + n_rows)
    display(timeline.iloc[start_entry_idx : end_entry_idx + 1]) # +1 to include the end_entry_idx
    print("\n")

    # --- Exit Date Analysis ---
    print(f"### Timeline around Exit Date: {exit_date} ({n_rows} rows before and after)\n")
    exit_loc = timeline.index.get_loc(exit_ts)
    start_exit_idx = max(0, exit_loc - n_rows)
    end_exit_idx = min(len(timeline) - 1, exit_loc + n_rows)
    display(timeline.iloc[start_exit_idx : end_exit_idx + 1]) # +1 to include the end_exit_idx
    print("\n")

Now we tabulate the trade table and examine the lens around the first entry and exit, as follows:

# trades and key trade data
n = 3               # number of trades to be displayed
print(f'xxx  first {n} trades with key trade data')
display(trades.head(n))
print("\n")

# timeline around entry and exit of the first trade
entry_date = '2022-07-19'
exit_date = '2022-09-16'
n_r = 1             # number of rows before/after entry/exit
lense_at_entry_exit(timeline, entry_date, exit_date, n_r)

We will get:

Our findings are as follows:

Entry

  • An entry signal is triggered at bar [i].
  • Close[i] is used as the entry price.
  • The position size at [i+1] equals cash[i-1] / close[i], meaning all available cash is used to open the position.

Exit

  • An exit signal is triggered at bar [j].
  • Close[j] is used as the exit price.
  • The position is fully closed, and cash[j+1] increases by the proceeds from the exit order.

By default, vbt executes trades at the close price of the bar where an effective signal occurs. Since the signal itself is generated at the bar’s close, this represents a somewhat theoretical execution scenario.

3. Using the Next Bar’s Open for Trading

Let’s move to a more realistic execution model.

A signal is generated at the close of a given bar, but it is far more realistic to execute the trade at the open of the next bar. This mirrors e.g. the workflow of a part-time trader using daily charts — analyzing the market after the close and placing market orders for the following day. In a reasonably liquid market, such orders are typically filled around the next day’s open.

In vbt, this behavior can be modeled by setting the price parameter to the open series, while shifting the open prices one bar backward.

The portfolio instance is then created as follows:

# baseline portfolio 
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    price=open.shift(-1),   # price to consider for trading (here: execution at next bar's open)
    init_cash=10_000,       # inital cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

Using the same code as presented above to display the trade table and the lens around the first entry and exit, we obtain:

We can clearly observe that positions are now opened and closed at the next bar’s open, which is the desired behavior for a more realistic execution model.

Note: We have demonstrated how to generate timeline DataFrames containing OHLC data, entry/exit signals, effective entries/exits, and how to extend the timeline with additional parameters of interest (e.g., indicators).

To keep the discussion concise, the upcoming sections will not include screenshots of trades or timelines. Instead, we will focus on compact descriptions of the portfolio’s behavior. You are encouraged to inspect the code and plot/prints on GitHub for verification.

4. Impact of Fees and Slippage

Fees

We can account for trading costs in two ways:

  • Fixed fees: a fixed cost per trade
  • Variable fees: a percentage of the position size

These parameters reflect the real costs associated with trading. The portfolio instance including fees is generated as follows:

# portfolio with fees (but no slippage)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    price=open.shift(-1),   # price to consider for trading (ensures execution at next open)
    fees=0.0025,            # 0.25% per order (!!!)
    fixed_fees=4.5,         # 4.5 EUR/order
    slippage=0,             # no slippage
    init_cash=10_000,       # inital cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

We can now compare the trade table with the one, generated without fees and observe:

  • The entry and exit prices still match the relevant open prices in the timeline (since price = open.shift(-1)).
  • The PnL values differ, fees are deducted from the profit/loss.

Note: As in real life, fees are applied per order — separately for entry and exit.

Slippage

In a realistic simulation, we also need to account for slippage — expressed as a percentage of the entry/exit price. Slippage reflects the fact that trades are rarely executed exactly at the intended price, often due to limited liquidity.

To model this, a “penalty” is applied to the trade price:

  • Long entry: price is increased by 1 + slippage
  • Exit: price is reduced by 1 - slippage

For highly liquid stocks, a slippage of 0.05% (0.0005) is usually adequate.

A portfolio-instance, considering slippage is generated as follows:

# Portfolio with slippage, no fees
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    price=open.shift(-1),   # price to consider for trading (ensures execution at next open)
    fees=0,                 # no fee
    fixed_fees=0,           # no fee
    slippage=0.01,          # 1% (just to make the impact clear)
    init_cash=10_000,       # inital cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

We can clearly observe the impact of slippage by comparing with the baseline portfolio:

  • Entry prices are higher
  • Exit prices are lower

As a result, the PnL is reduced compared to the baseline.

5. The Role of size, size_type and min_size

By default, size_type is set to 'Percent' and size is set to 1.0. This means:

  • A valid entry order uses 100% of the available cash
  • A valid exit order closes the position completely

Below, we generate the portfolio instance and record:

  • Portfolio value
  • Portfolio cash
  • Number of assets in the portfolio
# standard settings (size_type ='Percent' and size=1.0)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    size_type='Percent',    # order volume defined by percent of available cash
    size=1.0,               # 100% to be used
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

# recording results
value_100_pct = pf.value()
cash_100_pct = pf.cash()
assets_100_pct = pf.assets()

The parameter size controls the percentage of cash used for an entry. For example, if we set size = 0.3:

  • At a valid entry signal, 30% of the available cash is used to open the position
  • At a valid exit signal, the position is fully closed (!!)

For this portfolio instance, we will also record the portfolio value, cash, and number of assets after generation.

# modified settings (size_type ='Percent' and size=0.3)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    size_type='Percent',    # order volume defined by percent of available cash
    size=0.3,               # 30% to be used
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

# recording results
value_30_pct = pf.value()
cash_30_pct = pf.cash()
assets_30_pct = pf.assets()

If we plot the portfolio value, cash, and number of assets for the two portfolios, their differing behavior becomes immediately clear:

Other options :

For size_type we do have the following other options:

  • size_type = 'Amount' – the size parameter defines the number of assets (shares) to be traded
  • size_type = 'Value' – the size parameter defines the total entry value of the trade

You can explore the behavior of the portfolios in more detail on GitHub.

Exploring min_size and max_size

As a reference, we generate a portfolio instance using size_type = 'Percent' and size = 0.3.

In doing so, we obtain the following list of trades (9 trades in total):

For size_type = 'Percent', 'Amount', or 'Value', an order is executed only if there is enough cash to buy at least min_size number of assets.

Get Tobi Lux’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Note: min_size is always expressed as a number of assets, regardless of the chosen size_type. By inspecting the trade table, you can see that some orders may no longer be executed. By default, min_size is set to 1e-8.

Similarly, by setting max_size, you can limit the number of assets per order.

In our case we generated a new portfolio instance as follows:

# modified settings (size_type ='Value' and size=3_000, min number of assets = 20.0)
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                  # series (boolean) of exit-signals
    size_type='Value',      # order volume defined by value
    size=3_000,             # per order 3000 (EUR, USD,...) to be traded
    min_size = 20.0,        # minimum 20.0 of assets to be traded
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash (EUR, USD, ...)
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

As result we gets much less trades, since all trades with less than 20.0 assets are not executed (we get 3 trades in total):

6. TakeProfit Stops

First, we study TakeProfit stops in isolation, excluding system-based exits.

The TakeProfit stop limit can be set via tp_stop = value, where:

  • value defines the percentage of trade profit at which the position is closed
  • Example: tp_stop = 0.15 sets a 15% profit limit
  • In our example, the close price is used as the price reference
# generate a portfolio instance only with take-profit stops
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    # exits,                # no exits from the system
    tp_stop=0.15,           # take profit at 15%
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

When we inspect the timelines around entries and exits of a trade, we observe the following:

Entries

  • Entry signal is triggered at bar [i]
  • Entry date = date of bar [i]
  • Entry price = close[i] (the close of that bar)
  • Under the hood: the take-profit limit is calculated as (1 + tp_stop) * close[i] and is fixed for the trade

Exits

  • If close[j] exceeds the take-profit limit, the take-profit exit is triggered at bar [j]
  • Exit date = date of bar [j]
  • Exit price = close[j]

Note: close[j] may be higher than the take-profit limit, so the exact tp_stop value may not be perfectly achieved.

Let’s now use the next bar’s open price for trading and examine the behavior of the TakeProfit stop under this execution model:

# generate a portfolio instance only with take-profit stops
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    # exits,                # no exits from the system
    price=open.shift(-1),   # price to consider for trading (ensures execution at next open)
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
    tp_stop=0.15,           # take profit at 15%
)

When we inspect the trade tables and the timelines, we observe the following:

Entries

  • Entry signal is triggered at bar [i]
  • Entry date = date of bar [i]
  • Entry price = open[i+1] (next bar’s open)
  • Under the hood: the take-profit limit is calculated as (1 + tp_stop) * open[i+1] and is fixed for the trade

Exits

  • If close[j] exceeds the take-profit limit, the take-profit exit is triggered at bar [j]
  • Exit date = date of bar [j]
  • Exit price = close[j]

Note: Even though we set price = open.shift(-1), stops are executed at the close of the bar, not at the next bar’s open.
Also, close[j] may exceed the take-profit limit, so the exact tp_stop value may not be precisely met.

7. Advanced TakeProfit Stops

It can feel somewhat artificial to wait until the end of the bar to execute TakeProfit stops at the close.

In real trading, you know your entry price and can precisely calculate the take-profit limit — the price at which your trade profit reaches the tp_stop value. You would place a take-profit limit order at that level.

This order is filled intra-bar if any price exceeds the limit. In other words, if the high of a bar exceeds the take-profit limit, the stop is triggered immediately within the bar.

In vbt, we can mimic this behavior by providing the high series as a parameter when generating the portfolio instance:

# generate a portfolio instance only with take-profit stops, high series passed
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    # exits,                # no exits from the system
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
    tp_stop=0.15,           # take profit at 15%
    high=high,              # series of the high-prices from OHLC-data
)

Once inspecting the trade table and the timeline we observe:

For entries, the behavior remains the same:

  • Entry signal occurs at bar [i]
  • Entry date = date of bar [i], entry price = close[i]
  • Under the hood: the take-profit limit is calculated as (1 + tp_stop) * close[i]

For exits, we observe a different behavior compared to the previous simulation:

  • Take-profit exit conditions are fulfilled at bar [j] (in our example, this happens one bar earlier)
  • Exit date = date of bar [j]
  • Exit price: not directly found in OHLC data, but set to the take-profit limit calculated at entry

Note: As a result, the trade PnL is very close to the specified tp_stop value.

This approach effectively mimics the execution of a limit order intra-bar, triggered once the trade profit exceeds the tp_stop value.

8. StopLoss

We now study a StopLoss in isolation by disabling strategy-based exits and using only StopLoss exits.

We can set:

  • sl_stop=value (the percentage of acceptable loss until the stop is triggered)
  • sl_trail=True/False (controls trailing behavior)

If sl_trail is set to True, the StopLoss trails the price—it increases proportionally with rising prices but remains unchanged if the price declines.

The portfolio instance, using only a (trailing) StopLoss as the exit for positions, is generated as follows:

# generate a portfolio instance only with (trailing) stop-loss
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    # exits,                # no exits from the system
    sl_stop=0.05,           # stop loss at 5%
    sl_trail=True,          # trailing stop loss
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
)

By inspecting different scenarios, we observe the following:

Entry

  • Signal is triggered at bar [i]
  • Trade is executed at close[i] as the entry price
  • Under the hood, the initial StopLoss is calculated as (1 - sl_stop) * close[i]

During the Trade

  • The current (trailing) StopLoss is updated if prices increase and sl_trail = True; otherwise, it remains unchanged
  • Formula: trailing_price[i] = max((1 - sl_stop) * price[i], trailing_price[i-1])
  • In the current (default) setup, price = close

Exit

  • At bar [j], a (trailing) StopLoss signal is triggered when close[j] falls below the current trailing StopLoss
  • Exit price = close[j]

Note: It is currently a bit difficult to demonstrate the triggering of a StopLoss since its actual value is not directly accessible with the methods presented so far.

In the next section, we will show methods to access these values via callback functions and revisit the details of (trailing) StopLoss behavior.

9. Advanced StopLoss Techniques

We encounter a similar artificial situation as with TakeProfit stops. In reality, we do not wait until the close price drops below the (trailing) StopLoss to execute the StopLoss-order.

In real life, you know the (trailing) StopLoss for each bar and place a market StopLoss order accordingly. Once the price intrabar falls below that StopLoss, the exit order is immediately executed.

To mimic this behavior in vbt, we provide the high and low price series as arguments to the .from_signal(...) class method:

# generate a portfolio instance only with trailing Stop-Loss
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                  # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    # exits,                # no exits from the system
    init_cash=10_000,       # initial cash
    freq='D',               # all parameters on daily base
    sl_stop=0.05,           # stop loss at 5%
    sl_trail=True,          # trailing stop loss
    high=high,              # series of the high-prices from OHLC-data
    low=low,                # series of the low-prices from OHLC-data
)

Entry and During Trade

  • Usual behavior (as above)

Exits

  • At bar [j] a trailing-stop is triggered, when ???
  • Exit price is at ???

Note: If you check the TradeReport, you will find for values, that cannot be derived directly from the timeline-dataframe (here noted as ???). At a first glance, it is not possible to understand the mechanics of triggering the StopLoss form the available data. We will return to this in the next parts, when discussing callback functions and extracting the exact StopLoss and TakeProfit values.

For the time being (without evidence, but to be proven in later parts), the (trailing) StopLoss mechanism can be described as follows:

  • Trailing Stop formula: trailing_stop[j] = max((1 - sl_stop) * high[j-1], trailing_stop[j-1])
  • Trigger condition: trailing Stop-Loss at bar [j] is activated if low[j] < trailing_stop[j]

Thus, the (trailing) StopLoss is calculated using the highs (!!)(conservatively, elevating the triggering of a StopLoss signal to the highest reasonable level). The (trailing) StopLoss signal is triggered intrabar if the low of a bar falls below the (trailing) StopLoss at that specific bar.

10. Combining Exits from Strategy, Take-Profits, and Stop-Losses

We can now combine all different types of exits:

  • Exit triggered by a strategy-generated signal
  • Exit triggered by exceeding a TakeProfit limit
  • Exit triggered by breaching a (trailing) StopLoss

The portfolio instance is generated as follows:

# stop parameter
stop_loss = 0.05            # 5%
stop_loss_trail = True      # trailing-stop-loss
take_profit = 0.1          # 10%

# generate a portfolio instance with all three types of exits
pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(
    close,                      # price info (defines also the index of any timeline)
    entries,                    # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    exits,                      # series (boolean) of entry-signals
    sl_stop=stop_loss,          # stop loss at 5%
    sl_trail=stop_loss_trail,   # trailing stop loss
    tp_stop=take_profit,        # take profit at 15%
    init_cash=10_000,           # initial cash
    freq='D',                   # all parameters on daily base
)

Unfortunately, vbt does not provide information about the type of the effective exit in pf.trades.records_readable.

To address this, we need to use a customized function, get_exit_type(...), to identify whether an exit was triggered by a strategy signal, Take-Profit, or Stop-Loss:

def get_exit_type(row, tp, exits_series):
    exit_timestamp = row['Exit Timestamp']
    entry_timestamp = row['Entry Timestamp']
    percentage_change = (row['Avg Exit Price'] - row['Avg Entry Price']) / row['Avg Entry Price']

    if percentage_change >= tp:
        return 'take profit'
    # Change this line to use exits_series
    elif exit_timestamp in exits_series.index and exits_series.loc[exit_timestamp]:
        return 'system'
    else:
        return 'stop loss'

As a result we can generate the trades-table, supplemented by the exit type.

When generating the timeline, we will now add a flag for the position. We start by using the method pf.net_exposure(), which returns the ratio of 'value in position' to 'total value'.

By taking the sign of this ratio, we get:

  • 1 if the position is Long
  • 0 if the position is Flat
# list of trades with key data, amended by the trade type
trades = pf.trades.records_readable.copy()
trades['exit_type'] = trades.apply(get_exit_type, axis=1, args=(take_profit, exits))

# generating timeline for standard + specific trading parameter
timeline=get_timeline_extended(prices, entries, exits, trades)    # standards (price, entry, exits)
timeline['sma_fast'] = fast_sma                                   # add indicators
timeline['sma_slow'] = slow_sma
timeline['position'] = np.sign(pf.net_exposure())                 # adding position (1 = LONG, 0 = FLAT)

If we print trades, we see now a column with the type of exits.

In preparation for further analyses, we have also developed a very flexible plotting function, plot_multi_subplot_trading_data(...). The source code is not included here but can be inspected on GitHub.

This function leverages standard Plotly functionality and allows the creation of customized, flexible dashboards.

Below is the first example layout:

  • Upper plot: Candlestick price chart with indicators, entries, and exits (by type)
  • Lower plot: Position

Both functions (extension of the trade table and summary plot) are significant extension of vbt-standard outputs, since we can now evaluate/visualize the impact of the different types of exits.

Conclusion

We have now reached the end of Part 2. We have learned how to select the right trade-prices (default/modified) and have studied the impact of important parameters of portfolio.from_signals() as size_type, size, min_size, max_size, fees, slippage finally, we have studied the standard StopLoss and TakeProfit-behaviour. This foundation will serve as the basis for the next parts. Stay tuned!
